3294.name: Dak Prescott
3294.headline: Dak Prescott: bounce-back QB1 with top-five ceiling
3294.body:
Prescott reclaimed elite status in 2025, ranking third among quarterbacks with 313.8 fantasy points across a full 17 games after the 2024 ankle injury limited him to eight starts and a dismal QB31 finish. His 4,552 yards, 30 touchdowns and a 0.138 EPA per play confirmed the dip was health-driven, not decline, though his 47.4% success rate still trailed the 50% he posted at his 2023 peak. At 32, the efficiency floor is firmly established, and a 67.3% completion rate keeps the volume productive.

The surrounding talent only strengthens the case: a CeeDee Lamb, George Pickens and Jake Ferguson trio gives him a complete passing menu, and sharper red-zone conversion could push him toward the top five. He profiles as a draft-day bargain, with consensus rankings near QB11 and a price hovering around the eighth quarterback off the board. Modest passing-volume regression is plausible, but the discount makes him a near-lock value at QB1.

4866.name: Saquon Barkley
4866.headline: Aging Barkley a steady RB1 with diminished ceiling
4866.body:
Our model ranked Barkley the RB11 in 2025 at 195.3 fantasy points across 16 games, a clear step back from his RB1, 322.3-point monster of 2024. The volume stayed elite, but the explosion vanished: yards per carry fell sharply, his rushing scores were nearly halved, and his breakaway-run rate dropped. His EPA per play slipped negative, signaling the 2024 outlier season has corrected toward a more ordinary profile.

Now entering his age-29 season, he profiles as a high-floor RB1 rather than a league-winner. The workhorse role is secure, but an aging line and a career carry count climbing toward 2,000 invite caution. Expert boards back that read, placing him around RB7-8 with a top-15 floor; weigh him against younger backs before reaching early.

4881.name: Lamar Jackson
4881.headline: Lamar Jackson eyes elite QB1 rebound after injuries
4881.body:
Our advanced metrics tell two stories. In 2024 Jackson ranked sixth among quarterbacks with a monstrous 430.4 fantasy points across 17 games, posting a 0.284 EPA per play, 51.3% success rate, 41 passing touchdowns to four picks, and 915 rushing yards. Last season was a different animal: limited to 13 games, he slid to QB23 with just 214.9 points, 21 touchdowns, and his efficiency collapsed to a 0.067 EPA per play and 45.9% success rate, while rushing output cratered.

The gap is almost entirely health and a shrinking ground role rather than lost talent. A cascade of injuries gutted his trademark mobility and clouded his usage down the stretch. Still, drafters are treating the down year as noise, slotting him as a top-two quarterback in the early rounds. If his legs return, the dual-threat ceiling that produced an elite 2024 makes him a league-winning value.

4984.name: Josh Allen
4984.headline: Josh Allen stays the QB1 floor with rushing ceiling
4984.body:
Our model put Allen 11th among quarterbacks in 2025, a slip in positional rank that undersells the season: he set a personal best with a 69.3% completion rate, threw for 3,668 yards and 25 scores against just 10 picks, and added 579 yards and 14 touchdowns on the ground. That dual-threat profile delivered 364.6 fantasy points, the third straight year north of 360 after 392.6 in 2023 and 379 in 2024. His 0.171 EPA per play dipped from 2024's elite 0.311, and a 48.9% success rate signals modest passing-efficiency regression worth monitoring.

The rushing equity is the floor that makes him matchup-proof, with 500-plus yards and a dozen-plus scores on the ground in each of the last three seasons. A deeper receiver room should lift his aerial volume after years of thin perimeter help. Treat him as a back-half second-round pick in single-quarterback formats and the unquestioned first selection in superflex builds.

6786.name: CeeDee Lamb
6786.headline: Lamb stays a WR1 even with shrunken volume
6786.body:
Lamb's 2025 was a clear step down from his peak: limited to 13 games, he posted 117 targets, 75 catches, 1,077 yards and just 3 touchdowns, finishing as our WR10 with 125.9 fantasy points. That's a steep fall from the WR2 monster of 2023 (181 targets, 1,749 yards, 12 scores, 268.2 points) and even 2024's WR8 line. His target share dipped to 19.3%, yet a 0.248 EPA per play and 54.7% success rate show the efficiency held when he was on the field.

The context behind the dip matters more than the raw decline. George Pickens' arrival siphoned looks, and a stingier Dallas defense trimmed the offense's overall snap count, capping the ceiling Lamb once owned as the lone alpha. Still, with Prescott driving the passing game and a top-10 draft cost, the talent and floor remain elite. Treat him as a rock-solid WR1 whose blow-up upside is now shared, not gone.

7564.name: Ja'Marr Chase
7564.headline: Ja'Marr Chase: elite WR1 floor, Burrow-driven ceiling
7564.body:
Chase still graded as our WR4 in 2025, posting 125 catches on a commanding 185 targets for 1,412 yards and eight scores across 16 games, good for 188.6 fantasy points. The dip in efficiency was telling, though: his EPA per play fell from 0.44 to 0.224 and his success rate slid from 61.7% to 53%, almost entirely a function of unstable quarterback play rather than any decline in his game. His 30.4% target share actually rose, confirming he remains the unquestioned engine of this passing attack.

The blueprint sits one year back, when a healthy Joe Burrow fueled a 276-point, 17-touchdown campaign that finished as our overall WR1. Restore that battery and Chase's red-zone volume and yards-after-catch translate into league-winning weeks again. Consensus already treats him as a top-three overall pick and the position's premier option, and that price is justified. Draft him without hesitation as a foundational WR1 whose only real variable is Burrow's availability.

9221.name: Jahmyr Gibbs
9221.headline: Gibbs profiles as a true bell-cow ascendant
9221.body:
Gibbs has built one of the steadiest profiles at the position, ranking RB5 in our 2025 model after posting RB3 (310.9 points) in 2024. Last season he stacked 289.9 fantasy points across 17 games, splitting work as a runner and receiver: 243 carries for 1,223 yards at 5.03 a pop with 13 rushing scores, plus 77 grabs on 94 targets for 616 yards and five more touchdowns. The receiving floor is rare, and his red-zone opportunity total anchors the touchdown equity.

If the early-down committee that has capped his carries thins out, a path opens toward a true workhorse share in a high-powered Detroit attack. His passing-down role already spiked down the stretch, pushing a 17.1% target share that should climb further. Goal-line and red-zone volume both project up, and the dual-threat usage makes him a defensible first-overall selection in PPR formats.

9493.name: Puka Nacua
9493.headline: Puka Nacua's elite efficiency makes him a foundational WR1
9493.body:
Nacua sits as the No. 2 receiver in our advanced model after a dominant 2025: 129 catches on 166 targets for 1,715 yards and 10 touchdowns across 16 games, anchored by a 28.6% target share and a league-leading 3.71 yards per route run. The supporting metrics are absurd, a 0.697 EPA per play and a 70.5% success rate, and his 246 fantasy points led the position. Even his injury-shortened 2024, just 11 games and 990 yards, came with a career-high target rate, proving the volume share never wavers.

The Rams funnel everything through him, and with Matthew Stafford back under center he projects as the clear primary read once again. That floor explains why he carries a top-four consensus value and belongs in the opening tier of any draft. Health is the only real variable; the per-game production is too elite to fade, so treat him as a cornerstone pick.

9509.name: Bijan Robinson
9509.headline: Bijan Robinson is a locked-in 2026 RB1 anchor
9509.body:
Robinson graded as our overall RB1 in 2025, banking 291.8 fantasy points across all 17 games on a near-bell-cow snap share. He paired a heavy rushing workload with a 19.8% target share, 79 catches and 820 receiving yards, layering elite receiving production onto the ground game. That caps a steady climb from RB6 (188.3) as a rookie to RB4 (280.7) in 2024 to the top of the board in 2025.

The only real knock is touchdown variance; his rushing scores dipped even as total volume rose, a number that should normalize so long as the goal-line work stays largely his. The dual-threat floor is bulletproof regardless of game script, which is exactly what you want from a foundational pick. Drafted around the top two overall league-wide, he carries genuine RB1-overall upside if Atlanta finds the end zone more often.

11604.name: Brock Bowers
11604.headline: Bowers primed to reclaim the TE1 crown in 2026
11604.body:
Even compressed to 12 games by injury, Bowers finished as our TE10 in 2025, turning 86 targets into 64 grabs for 680 yards and seven scores while posting a stout 0.259 EPA per play and 112.2 fantasy points. Lean on the rookie blueprint instead: a TE1 finish in 2024 on 153 targets, 112 receptions, 1,194 yards and a commanding 25.8% target share that fueled 150.7 points. His 2025 share dipped to 17.4%, a function of missed time and a stalled attack rather than ability.

The roster math points back up. A thin receiver room leaves him the clear funnel for whoever wins the quarterback job, and nothing on the depth chart threatens his alpha role. Drafters are treating him as the position's top option around the second-third round turn, and that price is justified: healthy, he carries the highest weekly ceiling at a scarce position and a realistic path to overall TE1.
